topic: Types of Sentences and Interchange of Sentences
questionText:
Identify the type of sentence:

How efficiently the inspection team completed the verification.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (D) Exclamatory

• 'How efficiently the inspection team completed the verification.' — 'How' കൊണ്ട് തുടങ്ങുന്ന വാക്യം.
• Exclamatory sentence: How/What ഉപയോഗിച്ച് അതിശയം/പ്രശംസ/വികാരം പ്രകടിപ്പിക്കുന്നു.
• Pattern: 'How + adverb + subject + verb' = Exclamatory.
• Inspection team-ന്റെ efficiency-ക്ക് admiration = exclamatory.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula:
  How + adj/adv + S + V = Exclamatory
  What + (a/an) + adj + noun + S + V = Exclamatory
• Types of Sentences:
  - Assertive (Declarative): statement — 'She works hard.'
  - Interrogative: question — 'Does she work hard?'
  - Imperative: command/request — 'Work hard!'
  - Exclamatory: emotion/surprise — 'How hard she works!'
• Signal words: How... / What... (exclamatory, not interrogative)
• Difference: 'How efficiently?' (interrogative) vs 'How efficiently she works!' (exclamatory)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Exclamatory sentence identification — 'How/What' pattern PSC-ൽ regular.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. How beautiful this painting is!
  2. What a wonderful opportunity this is!
  3. How efficiently the team completed the task!
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) Assertive
• Assertive = plain statement of fact. 'How efficiently...' = emotion/admiration, not a plain statement.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Sentence looks like a statement (no '?') — students mark Assertive.

(B) Imperative
• Imperative = command/request (usually starts with a verb). 'How efficiently...' doesn't command.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Imperative and Exclamatory both often lack a stated subject — students confuse.

(C) Interrogative
• Interrogative = question. 'How' questions: 'How did they do it?' But 'How efficiently...' = admiration, no answer expected.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'How' often starts questions — but 'How + adj/adv + S + V' (no auxiliary before subject) = Exclamatory, not interrogative.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'HOW + quality + subject = WOW! = Exclamatory!'
  'How beautiful!' / 'How efficiently!' — expressing WOW = Exclamatory.
• Quick test: Does it ask a question? No → not Interrogative. Does it command? No → not Imperative. Does 'How/What' express amazement? Yes → Exclamatory!
• PSC tip: 'How + adjective/adverb' + subject + verb (no auxiliary flip) = always Exclamatory.

questionText:
Choose the correct option:

The delegation travelled by ______ air due to the urgency of the meeting.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (D) no article

• 'Travel by air' — modes of transport preposition phrase: 'by + transport' = NO article.
• 'Travel by air / by train / by bus / by sea / by road' — all zero article.
• Adding 'the' changes meaning: 'by the air' = wrong idiom.
• General rule: 'by + transport noun' = zero article (fixed phrase).
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: BY + transport noun = NO article (zero article)
  by air | by train | by bus | by car | by sea | by road | by rail | by boat | by plane
• Article use with transport:
  - 'Take A bus' (one specific trip = a/an) ✓
  - 'Travel by bus' (mode of transport = no article) ✓
  - 'The bus' (specific bus you mentioned before = the) ✓
• 'By air' = using air travel as a mode — fixed idiom, no article.
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Zero article with 'by + transport' — PSC-ൽ regular.
• Similar zero article patterns:
  - By profession: 'She is a doctor BY profession'
  - In/at/on + location: 'at home, in hospital, at school, in bed'
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The delegation travelled by air to reach on time.
  2. He goes to work by train every day.
  3. They prefer travelling by sea rather than by air.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) a
• 'By a air' — 'air' starts with a vowel, so 'a' is doubly wrong (should be 'an' anyway).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Air' has vowel sound → students pick 'an', not 'a' — but more importantly, no article needed.

(B) an
• 'By an air' — 'an' before vowel sound correct form, but 'by air' = fixed phrase, no article at all.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Air' starts with vowel → 'an air' feels right. But 'by + transport' = zero article.

(C) the
• 'By the air' — 'the' makes it specific/definite. 'By the air' = not standard English. 'By air' (zero article) = correct idiom.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'The' feels definite and proper — but 'by air' is a fixed idiom that never takes 'the'.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'BY + transport = NO article! By air, by train, by bus — all bare!'
• List to memorize: by air · by train · by bus · by car · by sea · by road · by rail · by bicycle · by taxi
• Exception: 'by the side of', 'by the time' — 'the' used with non-transport. Transport = always zero!

questionText:
Choose the correct verb form:

Neither the training module nor the supplementary material ______ available on the portal.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) is

• 'Neither the training module NOR the supplementary material _____ available'
• Neither...nor rule: verb agrees with the subject NEAREST to it (supplementary material = singular).
• 'Supplementary material' = singular → verb = 'is'.
• Neither A nor B = B determines verb form.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula — Correlative conjunctions:
  Neither A nor B → verb agrees with B (nearest subject)
  Either A or B → verb agrees with B (nearest subject)
  Not only A but also B → verb agrees with B
• Examples:
  - Neither the boys nor the girl IS here. (girl = singular → is)
  - Neither the girl nor the boys ARE here. (boys = plural → are)
  - Neither the module nor the material IS available. (material = singular → is)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Neither...nor agreement — most tested agreement rule in PSC.
• Other agreement rules:
  - A and B = plural verb (and = addition)
  - Each/Every/Either/Neither + singular noun = singular verb
  - A as well as B = verb agrees with A (first subject)
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. Neither he nor his friends were present.
  2. Neither the students nor the teacher was ready.
  3. Either the manager or the staff members are responsible.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(B) are
• 'Are' = plural. 'Supplementary material' (nearest subject) = singular → 'is' needed.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Two things mentioned (module + material) → students use plural verb. But neither...nor = nearest subject rule.

(C) were
• 'Were' = past plural — tense mismatch (sentence is present) + number mismatch.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Were' = past form of 'are' — students who wrongly pick plural also pick wrong tense.

(D) have been
• 'Have been' = Present Perfect plural. Wrong tense and wrong number.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Have been available' sounds formal/complete — but wrong on both tense and agreement.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'Neither...NOR → look at what comes AFTER NOR — that's your verb guide!'
  Neither A nor B = B rules the verb.
• Simple: Cover 'Neither X nor' with your hand. What remains? 'The supplementary material ___' = singular = IS.
• PSC pattern: Neither...nor / Either...or = PROXIMITY RULE — nearest subject wins every time!

questionText:
Choose the correct question tag:

Let us reconsider the proposal, ______ ?

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) shall we

• 'Let us reconsider the proposal, _____?'
• 'Let us' = suggestion/invitation (Let's) → tag = 'shall we?'
• This is a fixed rule: Let's/Let us + V → shall we?
• 'Let's go, shall we?' — same pattern.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Let's / Let us → shall we?
• Special question tag rules:
  - Positive statement → Negative tag
  - Negative statement → Positive tag
  - Imperative (do/don't) → will you?
  - Let's / Let us → shall we?
  - Please + imperative → will you?
• 'Let us' = first person plural suggestion. The invitation tag = 'shall we?' (asking if group agrees).
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'Let's...shall we?' — classic PSC question tag tested every year.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. Let's start the meeting, shall we?
  2. Let us go for a walk, shall we?
  3. Let's try a different approach, shall we?
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(B) will you
• 'Will you?' = tag for imperative commands ('Open the door, will you?'). 'Let us' is not a command.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Imperative and 'Let us' both suggest action → students use 'will you?' for both.

(C) do we
• 'Do we?' = tag for present tense questions. Not applicable for 'Let us' constructions.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'We' in 'let us' → 'do we?' feels like a logical match — but wrong tag type.

(D) don't we
• 'Don't we?' = negative tag for positive present statements with 'we'. Not for 'Let us'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Let us' → 'we' → 'don't we?' — surface pronoun match but wrong tag rule.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'LET'S = SHALL WE? — Let's go, shall we? Let us try, shall we? Always SHALL WE!'
• Tag chart (PSC must-know):
  Let's → shall we? | Imperative → will you? | I am → aren't I?
• PSC shortcut: See 'Let us / Let's' → immediately write 'shall we?' — no other option is correct.

questionText: Identify the correct sentence:
optionA: The director had the assistant to prepare the summary.
optionB: The director had the assistant prepare the summary.
optionC: The director had the assistant prepared the summary.
optionD: The director had the assistant preparing the summary.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) The director had the assistant prepare the summary.

• 'Have + object + bare infinitive (V1)' = causative structure.
• 'Had the assistant PREPARE' (bare infinitive, no 'to') = correct causative.
• Meaning: Director caused/arranged for the assistant to prepare it.
• 'Had + person + V1 (base form)' = standard causative 'have'.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: have/get + object + verb form:
  HAVE + person + V1 (bare infinitive): 'She had him fix the car.'
  GET + person + TO + V1: 'She got him to fix the car.'
  HAVE + thing + V3 (past participle): 'She had the car fixed.'
• Causative 'have' pattern:
  have + someone + do (not to do, not doing, not did)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Causative 'have/get' — PSC-ൽ correction of sentences regular.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. She had the electrician repair the fan. (bare infinitive)
  2. He got the mechanic to fix the car. (to + infinitive)
  3. They had the report prepared by the team. (thing + V3)
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) The director had the assistant to prepare the summary.
• 'To prepare' after 'had + person' = wrong. Causative 'have' uses bare infinitive (prepare, not to prepare).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'To prepare' sounds natural in English (like 'asked to prepare') — but 'have' causative ≠ 'to'.

(C) The director had the assistant prepared the summary.
• 'Prepared' = past tense — makes it a confusing double past. 'Had prepared' = Past Perfect (different meaning).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Prepared' = V3 — students confuse causative 'have + thing + V3' with 'have + person + V3'.

(D) The director had the assistant preparing the summary.
• 'Preparing' = present participle — 'have + person + V-ing' is not standard causative.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: '-ing' form feels active/ongoing — but causative 'have + person' needs bare infinitive.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'HAVE + PERSON + BARE VERB — no TO, no -ED, no -ING!'
  'Had the assistant PREPARE' (bare) ✓ | 'Had the assistant to prepare' ✗
• Causative table:
  have + person + V1 (bare) = 'She had him clean the office.'
  get + person + to + V1 = 'She got him to clean the office.'
• PSC tip: See 'had/have + person' → next verb = bare infinitive (V1) — remove 'to', remove '-ing', keep bare!

questionText: Ravi ______ tea every morning.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) drinks

• 'Ravi _____ tea EVERY MORNING' — 'every morning' = habitual/routine action signal.
• Simple Present tense: Ravi drinks tea every morning.
• Third person singular (Ravi = he) → verb + 's' = drinks.
• Habit/routine = Simple Present, not continuous or perfect.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: 'Every + time' signal → Simple Present (habit/routine)
• Habitual action signal words:
  every day/morning/week | always | usually | often | sometimes | rarely | never | regularly
• Simple Present — third person singular (he/she/it/name) → verb + s/es:
  drink → drinks | go → goes | watch → watches | teach → teaches
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'Every morning' = Simple Present — PSC guaranteed question.
• Tense signals:
  - 'every day' → Simple Present
  - 'now/at the moment' → Present Continuous
  - 'yesterday/last year' → Simple Past
  - 'just/already/yet' → Present Perfect
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. She drinks coffee every morning.
  2. He goes to the gym every day.
  3. The train arrives at 7 a.m. every morning.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) is drinking
• Present Continuous — for actions happening NOW at this moment, not habitual.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Is drinking' sounds active/ongoing — but 'every morning' = habit = Simple Present.

(C) drank
• Simple Past — for completed past action. 'Every morning' is ongoing habit, not past.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Drank' (past) sounds definite — students pick it thinking past is more 'sure'.

(D) has drunk
• Present Perfect — for experiences/recent results. 'Every morning' habitual ≠ Present Perfect.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Has drunk' = completed action feel. But habit = Simple Present, not Perfect.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'EVERY = ALWAYS = SIMPLE PRESENT!' Every + time word = Simple Present. No exceptions.
• Third person rule: Ravi (=he) + Simple Present = add 's' → drinks (not drink).
• PSC guaranteed: 'Every morning/day/week' → Simple Present → check if 3rd person → add 's'. Done in 3 seconds!

questionText: The plant shed its leaves. (Identify the Possessive Pronoun)

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (C) its

• 'The plant shed ITS leaves' — 'its' = possessive pronoun/adjective (belonging to the plant).
• 'Its' = showing possession for 'it' (plant = it → its leaves).
• Possessive pronouns: my, your, his, her, its, our, their.
• 'Its' here = possessive determiner (possessive adjective — modifies 'leaves').
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Possessive pronouns = my/your/his/her/its/our/their/mine/yours/his/hers/ours/theirs
• Two types:
  - Possessive adjectives (before noun): my book, his pen, its leaves
  - Possessive pronouns (stand alone): This book is mine. That pen is his.
• 'Its' = possessive of 'it' (no apostrophe! 'it's' = it is)
• Parts of Speech:
  - Noun: person, place, thing, idea (plant, leaves)
  - Verb: action/state (shed)
  - Pronoun: replaces noun (its)
  - Adjective: describes noun (The)
  - Article: the, a, an (special adjective)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Identify parts of speech — PSC-ൽ identify pronouns regular.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The tree shed its bark in summer.
  2. The cat licked its paw carefully.
  3. The company lost its license.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) plant
• 'Plant' = noun (common noun, subject of sentence). Not a pronoun.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Plant' is the main word in the sentence — students identify it as most important.

(B) shed
• 'Shed' = verb (action — the plant performed 'shedding'). Not a pronoun.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Shed' can be a noun too (a shed = outbuilding) — parts of speech confusion.

(D) The
• 'The' = definite article (a type of adjective/determiner). Not a pronoun.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'The' is a word class that students sometimes confuse — it's an article, not a pronoun.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'ITS = IT'S possession! The plant → it → ITS leaves. No apostrophe needed for possession!'
  'its' (possession) vs 'it's' (it is) — common PSC error!
• Possessive pronoun hunt: Look for words that show OWNERSHIP — whose leaves? The plant's = its.
• PSC tip: When asked 'identify the possessive pronoun' → find the word showing ownership (whose?) → 'its'.

questionText: Keerthana won't be late, ______? (Use suitable question tag)

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) will she

• 'Keerthana won't be late, _____?'
• Negative statement → Positive tag.
• Auxiliary in statement: 'won't' = will + not → positive tag = 'will'.
• Subject: Keerthana = she.
• Tag: 'will she?' (positive, present future auxiliary).
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നிയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula:
  Negative statement → Positive tag
  'won't' → extract 'will' → positive tag = 'will'
• Negative auxiliary → positive tag:
  won't → will | isn't → is | aren't → are | wasn't → was | haven't → have | can't → can
• Subject: Keerthana → pronoun = she
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Negative statement tags with 'won't' — PSC-ൽ regular.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. She won't come, will she?
  2. He can't drive, can he?
  3. They haven't finished, have they?
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(B) will not she
• Tag should be contracted: 'will she' not 'will not she'. Positive tags never have 'not'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Won't' in statement → students think tag should also have negative 'not' — but polarity flips!

(C) would she
• 'Would' = wrong auxiliary. Statement uses 'will' (won't = will + not) → tag = 'will', not 'would'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Would' sounds more polite/formal — students upgrade tense unnecessarily.

(D) wouldn't she
• 'Wouldn't she?' = negative tag — but negative statement needs POSITIVE tag. Double wrong: wrong auxiliary + wrong polarity.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Statement is negative → students pick another negative tag — most common tag error!
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'WON'T = WILL + NOT → strip NOT → add to tag = WILL SHE! Negative flips to positive!'
• Two steps: ① Find auxiliary in statement (won't → will) ② Flip polarity (negative → positive) → will she?
• Never use 'not' in a positive tag. Tags are: will she? ✓ | will not she? ✗

questionText: Now we shall discuss ______ our future prospects.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (D) no preposition

• 'Discuss _____ our future prospects' — 'discuss' is a transitive verb that takes a direct object WITHOUT a preposition.
• 'We shall discuss our future prospects' — direct object, no preposition.
• 'Discuss about' = incorrect in formal English. 'Discuss' already implies 'about'.
• Common error: 'discuss about' — redundant, wrong.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: DISCUSS + object (no 'about'!) — 'about' is already implied
• Transitive verbs that take DIRECT objects (no preposition):
  - Discuss (not discuss about)
  - Mention (not mention about)
  - Consider (not consider about)
  - Comprise (not comprise of)
  - Approach (not approach to)
  - Enter (not enter into — in most contexts)
  - Reach (not reach to)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'Discuss about' error — PSC most tested preposition mistake!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. We discussed the matter thoroughly. ✓ (not 'discussed about')
  2. She mentioned the problem to her boss. ✓ (not 'mentioned about')
  3. Let us discuss our plans for next year. ✓
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) about
• 'Discuss about' = most common grammar error. 'About' is redundant — 'discuss' already means 'talk about'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Talk about' is correct → students apply same pattern to 'discuss about' — wrong!

(B) of
• 'Discuss of' — no such collocation in standard English.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Random preposition distractor.

(C) for
• 'Discuss for' — no such collocation.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Hope for / prepare for' → students transfer 'for' pattern to 'discuss' — wrong.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'DISCUSS = TALK ABOUT — so adding ABOUT again = double meaning = WRONG!'
  'Discuss + object' (no about) | 'Talk about + object' (needs about)
• Preposition-free verbs: DISCUSS · MENTION · CONSIDER · COMPRISE · APPROACH · REACH — memorize this list!
• PSC tip: 'Discuss about' appears in options → the answer is 'no preposition'. This is almost always the trap!

questionText: We are going to bake the bread. (Change into Passive Voice)
optionA: The bread is going to be baked.
optionB: The bread was going to be baked.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) The bread is going to be baked.

• Active: 'We ARE GOING TO BAKE the bread.' — 'be going to' future.
• Passive of 'be going to': is/am/are + going to + be + V3.
• 'Is going to be baked' = correct passive form.
• Object (bread) → Subject. 'We' dropped (no 'by us' needed — general agent).
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: be going to → be going to be + V3 (passive)
  Active: am/is/are + going to + V1
  Passive: am/is/are + going to + be + V3
• Full 'going to' conversion:
  Active: We are going to bake the bread.
  Passive: The bread is going to be baked (by us).
• Key: 'be' inserted before V3 in passive.
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'Going to' future passive — PSC-ൽ modal/future passive regular.
• Related passive forms:
  - will bake → will be baked
  - is going to bake → is going to be baked
  - is able to bake → is able to be baked
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. They are going to build a new school. → A new school is going to be built.
  2. We are going to announce the results. → The results are going to be announced.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(B) The bread was going to be baked.
• 'Was' = past — active sentence 'are going to' = present/future. Tense mismatch.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Was going to' feels like completed/past plan — but active tense is present 'are'.

(C) The bread has been baked.
• 'Has been baked' = Present Perfect passive. Active sentence ≠ Present Perfect structure.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Has been baked' = common passive form — but changes tense entirely.

(D) The bread will be baked.
• 'Will be baked' = Simple Future passive. Active is 'going to' (not simple 'will').
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Will be baked' looks like correct future passive — but 'be going to' ≠ 'will' exactly.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'GOING TO bake → GOING TO BE baked — just slide BE before the past participle!'
  be going to + V1 → be going to + BE + V3
• Tense lock rule: Active tense = Passive tense. 'Are going to' (present) → 'is going to be' (present). Never change tense!
• Pattern: am/is/are going to + V1 → am/is/are going to + be + V3. Insert 'be' — that's the only change.

questionText:
Pick out the antonym of the underlined word

The rules and regulations were __strictly__ enforced.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (D) lightly

• 'Strictly enforced' — 'strictly' = കർശനമായി, ശക്തമായി.
• Antonym of 'strictly' = 'lightly' = ലഘുവായി, loosely, without force.
• 'Lightly enforced' = not enforced seriously — opposite of strictly.
• 'Harshly/firmly/rigidly' are all SYNONYMS of strictly — wrong direction.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Strictly (strong enforcement) ↔ Lightly (weak/loose enforcement)
• 'Strictly' adverb family:
  strictly → strictly, rigidly, firmly, harshly, severely (synonyms)
  strictly ↔ lightly, loosely, mildly, gently (antonyms)
• Antonym = OPPOSITE meaning — not same meaning.
• Context: 'rules strictly enforced' = enforced with full force ↔ 'rules lightly enforced' = barely enforced.
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Antonym with underlined word — PSC-ൽ antonym/synonym regular section.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The law was strictly enforced in urban areas but lightly enforced in rural ones.
  2. She strictly followed the diet but he lightly followed it.
  3. The regulations are strictly imposed, not lightly dismissed.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) harshly
• 'Harshly' = cruelly, severely — synonym of 'strictly'. Same direction, not antonym.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Harshly' sounds like it could be different — but both mean strong enforcement.

(B) firmly
• 'Firmly' = steadfastly, solidly — synonym of 'strictly' (firm enforcement = strict enforcement).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Firmly' feels slightly different from 'strictly' — but still means strong application.

(C) rigidly
• 'Rigidly' = inflexibly — near synonym of 'strictly'. Not antonym.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Rigid' vs 'strict' — students think these are different enough to be antonyms. They're not!
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'Strictly = HEAVY force → Antonym = LIGHT force = LIGHTLY!'
  Strictly (heavy) ↔ Lightly (light). The word 'lightly' literally means the opposite!
• Elimination strategy: Antonym = OPPOSITE. Harshly/firmly/rigidly = all heavy/strong = same side as 'strictly'. Eliminate all three → only 'lightly' left.
• PSC pattern: Antonym questions — check which options are synonyms first, eliminate them, pick what's left.

topic: Singular & Plural, Change of Gender, Collective Nouns
questionText:
Fill up using the appropriate word from the brackets

A _______ of cattle lying under the tree

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) herd

• 'A _____ of CATTLE' — collective noun for cattle = 'herd'.
• 'A herd of cattle' = standard English collective noun.
• 'Herd' is used for large grazing animals: cattle, elephants, horses, deer.
• 'Cattle' = cows/bulls/oxen collectively → herd.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Collective noun + 'of' + animal group:
  herd of: cattle, elephants, horses, deer, buffaloes
  flock of: birds, sheep, chickens, ducks
  swarm of: bees, ants, locusts, flies
  fleet of: ships, planes, cars, vehicles
  pack of: wolves, dogs, cards
  pride of: lions
  school/shoal of: fish
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Collective nouns — PSC-ൽ very regular. 'Herd of cattle' is the most tested.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. A herd of cattle was grazing in the field.
  2. A flock of birds flew across the sky.
  3. A swarm of bees attacked the tree.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) flock
• 'Flock' = collective noun for birds or sheep — NOT cattle.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Flock' is common collective noun → students apply it to cattle. Wrong animal group.

(C) fleet
• 'Fleet' = collective noun for vehicles/ships/planes — not animals.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Fleet' sounds authoritative — but it's for vehicles, not living creatures.

(D) swarm
• 'Swarm' = collective noun for insects (bees, ants, locusts) — not cattle.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Swarm' feels like 'large group moving together' — cattle do move together but 'swarm' = insects only.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'HERD of cows HEARD mooing — HERD = big grazing animals!'
  Herd: cattle/elephants/horses. Flock: birds/sheep. Swarm: insects. Fleet: vehicles.
• PSC top 5 collective nouns to memorize:
  herd (cattle) · flock (birds) · swarm (bees) · pride (lions) · pack (wolves)
• Quick test: Is it a large land mammal? → HERD. Is it a bird? → FLOCK. Is it an insect? → SWARM.

questionText:
Fill up the blank with the correct phrasal verb.

The meeting was _______ due to the Manager's illness.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) put off

• 'The meeting was _____ due to the Manager's illness' — meeting postponed because of illness.
• 'Put off' = postpone/delay — same meaning as 43-2026-M_q82!
• 'Owing to illness' = reason for postponement.
• 'Was put off' = passive form of 'put off' (meeting was postponed).
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Put OFF = postpone/delay
• 'PUT' phrasal verbs:
  - Put off = postpone ('put off the meeting')
  - Put on = wear / organize an event
  - Put up = accommodate / tolerate ('put up with')
  - Put out = extinguish / publish
  - Put in = insert / submit
• Context: 'due to Manager's illness' = reason for delay → put off = postpone.
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'Put off' = postpone — appears in MULTIPLE papers. PSC favourite!
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The match was put off due to heavy rain.
  2. The seminar was put off when the speaker fell ill.
  3. Don't put off until tomorrow what you can do today.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) put on
• 'Put on' = wear/organize. 'Meeting put on' = could mean organized, not postponed — wrong context.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Put on an event' is possible meaning — but 'due to illness' signals postpone, not organize.

(C) put up
• 'Put up' = accommodate/tolerate ('put up with someone'). Not used for scheduling.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Put up with' the delay feels logical — but 'put up the meeting' is not standard.

(D) put out
• 'Put out' = extinguish (fire) or publish. Cannot postpone a meeting.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Put out' sounds final/decisive — students pick it when something is stopped.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'PUT OFF = push it OFF the calendar to later! Illness → postpone → put off!'
• Context keywords: 'due to / because of / owing to' + illness/rain/issue → always PUT OFF (postpone).
• PSC pattern: Put off = postpone appears repeatedly. If asked about meetings/events being delayed → PUT OFF, always!

questionText:
Replace the word underlined with its synonym.

Boby's success was the result of __perseverance__

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) persistence

• 'Perseverance' = ക്ഷമയോടെ ഒരു കാര്യം തുടർന്നുകൊണ്ടിരിക്കൽ — continuing despite difficulty.
• 'Persistence' = same meaning — keeping on doing something despite obstacles.
• Both words: not giving up, continuing through challenges.
• Boby succeeded because of persistence/perseverance = he kept trying.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Perseverance = Persistence = never giving up + continuing despite difficulty
• Word family:
  - Persevere (v) → Perseverance (n) → Persevering (adj)
  - Persist (v) → Persistence (n) → Persistent (adj)
• Synonym group:
  perseverance = persistence, tenacity, determination, diligence, endurance, steadfastness
• Antonym:
  perseverance ↔ giving up, surrender, laziness, inconsistency
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'Perseverance' synonym — PSC motivational vocabulary tested regularly.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. His perseverance in studies led to his success in PSC.
  2. She showed great persistence despite repeated failures.
  3. Perseverance is the key to achieving difficult goals.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) intelligence
• 'Intelligence' = mental ability — different quality from perseverance (continuing effort).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Success → intelligence feels like the cause. But sentence says perseverance = success.

(C) talent
• 'Talent' = natural ability/gift — also different from perseverance (which = effort, not natural ability).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Talent' and 'perseverance' both lead to success — but they're different words.

(D) luck
• 'Luck' = chance/fortune — complete opposite of perseverance (which = hard work over time).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Luck' appears in 'success' context — students might think success = luck, but sentence = perseverance.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'PERSEVERANCE = PERSIST + ANCE = PERSistence — same root PER-SIST-ENCE!'
  Both come from Latin 'persistere' = to stand firm. Identical meaning.
• Elimination: Intelligence = brain, Talent = gift, Luck = chance — none of these = 'keep trying'. Only Persistence = Perseverance.
• PSC vocabulary pair: perseverance ↔ persistence — learn as synonyms. Both = continuing despite difficulty.

questionText: Pick out the correct prefix to get the opposite of the word __manage__.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) mis

• 'Mismanage' = badly manage / manage incorrectly.
• Prefix 'mis-' = wrongly, badly, incorrectly.
• 'Mis + manage = mismanage' — opposite/negative form through wrong-doing.
• 'Mismanage' means to manage poorly or incorrectly.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: MIS- = wrongly/badly (action done incorrectly)
• Prefix meanings:
  - mis- = wrongly/badly: mismanage, misguide, misuse, mislead, misunderstand, misjudge
  - un- = not/reverse: unhappy, undo, unfair, unfinished
  - dis- = not/opposite: disagree, dishonest, disappear, disconnect
  - non- = not: nonviolent, nonstandard, nonprofit
• Why 'mis' for manage:
  'Manage' is an action → doing it wrongly = 'mis' + manage
  'Mismanage' = manage in a bad/wrong way
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Prefix identification — PSC Word Formation regular section.
• Word families with 'mis-':
  mismanage | misuse | mislead | misguide | misspell | mistake | misunderstand
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The funds were mismanaged, leading to losses.
  2. Don't misguide the students with wrong information.
  3. She misunderstood the instructions.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(B) un
• 'Unmanage' — not a standard English word. 'Un-' reverses actions or describes states.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Un-' is common negative prefix → students try 'unmanage' — but it doesn't exist.

(C) dis
• 'Dismanage' — not a word. 'Dis-' = opposite/separation (disagree, disconnect) but not for 'manage'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Dis-' and 'mis-' both make negatives — students confuse them.

(D) non
• 'Nonmanage' — not a word. 'Non-' = not (nonviolent) but not for verb actions like 'manage'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Non-' sounds formal/official — students pick it for formal words like 'manage'.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'MIS = MISTAKE = doing it WRONG! MISmanage = manage it wrong!'
  mis = wrongly: mismanage · misuse · mislead · misspell · mistake
• Prefix guide:
  mis (wrong) + manage = mismanage ✓
  dis (not) + manage = dismanage ✗ (not a word)
  un (not) + manage = unmanage ✗ (not a word)
• PSC: For VERB + opposite meaning of doing wrongly = always MIS-.

questionText: What is the meaning of "Bona fide"?

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) In good faith / genuine

• 'Bona fide' = Latin — 'bona' (good) + 'fide' (faith).
• Meaning: genuine, authentic, in good faith — without deception.
• 'A bona fide request' = genuine request, not fake.
• 'Bona fide' is now used as an English adjective: 'a bona fide expert'.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Bona fide = genuine/authentic (Latin: good + faith)
• Latin phrases PSC must-know:
  - Bona fide = in good faith, genuine
  - De facto = in reality/practice (as opposed to legally)
  - De jure = by law/right
  - Sine die = without fixed date (indefinitely postponed)
  - Prima facie = at first sight/appearance
  - Status quo = existing state of affairs
  - Per capita = per person
  - Viva voce = oral examination
  - Post mortem = examination after death
  - Alma mater = school/university one attended
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Latin/foreign phrases — PSC every paper. Bona fide is most tested.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. He is a bona fide candidate for the position.
  2. The certificate must be a bona fide document.
  3. She made a bona fide effort to resolve the issue.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(B) Illegal
• 'Illegal' = against the law — opposite of bona fide (which = genuine/legal/authentic).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Fide' (faith/trust) confused with 'illegal/suspicious' — students pick extreme opposite.

(C) Temporary
• 'Temporary' = lasting for a short time — unrelated to 'bona fide'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Sine die' = indefinite → 'temporary' feel. But this question is about 'bona fide'.

(D) Immediate
• 'Immediate' = without delay — no relation to 'good faith/genuine'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Bona' sounds like 'bonus' (extra/quick) — students make phonetic association.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'BONA FIDE = BOND of FAITH = trust + genuine!' Fide = faith (like 'fidelity' = faithfulness).
  'Bona fide' = you can trust this — it's GENUINE.
• Latin root: fide = faith/trust (also in: fidelity, confide, confidential, Fido the loyal dog!)
• PSC tip: 'Bona fide' = genuine/authentic/in good faith. Whenever asked, one answer: GENUINE. Done!

topic: Expansion and Meaning of Common Abbreviations
questionText: What is the correct expansion of "LASER"?
optionA: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission Radiation
optionB: Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation
optionC: Light Amplified by Stimulated Emitted Radiation
optionD: Light Amplification through Stimulated Emission of Rays

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation

• LASER = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation.
• Key word: 'of' before 'Radiation' — option A misses 'of'.
• Full form: L=Light, A=Amplification, S=Stimulated, E=Emission, R=Radiation.
• 'Laser' is now a common noun (laser pointer, laser surgery).
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: LASER = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission OF Radiation
  (A vs B difference: Option A misses 'of' before 'Radiation')
• Common PSC Abbreviations/Acronyms:
  - LASER = Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation
  - RADAR = Radio Detection And Ranging
  - SONAR = Sound Navigation And Ranging
  - AIDS = Acquired Immune Deficiency Syndrome
  - VIRUS = Vital Information Resources Under Siege (backronym)
  - NATO = North Atlantic Treaty Organization
  - UNESCO = United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization
  - UNICEF = United Nations International Children's Emergency Fund
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തනം: Abbreviation expansions — PSC-ൽ LASER/RADAR/SONAR very regular.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. LASER technology is used in modern surgery.
  2. The RADAR system detected the incoming aircraft.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission Radiation
• Missing 'of' before Radiation — 'Emission Radiation' is wrong, should be 'Emission OF Radiation'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Looks almost identical to correct answer — only 'of' missing. Careful reading needed!

(C) Light Amplified by Stimulated Emitted Radiation
• 'Amplified' (past participle) wrong — should be 'Amplification' (noun). Also 'Emitted Radiation' wrong.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Verb form confusion — 'amplification' (process/noun) vs 'amplified' (adjective/past).

(D) Light Amplification through Stimulated Emission of Rays
• 'Through' instead of 'by', and 'Rays' instead of 'Radiation' — both wrong.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Rays' of light = logical association with laser light — but official expansion = 'Radiation'.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'LASER = L-A-S-E-R: Light And Sound Emit Rays? NO! Light Amplification by Stimulated Emission of Radiation!'
• The trap word: 'of' — Option A drops 'of'. Always check: Emission OF Radiation (not just 'Emission Radiation').
• PSC abbreviation family: LASER · RADAR · SONAR — all end in similar physics terms. Know the differences!

questionText: What is the one-word substitute for "a person who believes in the doctrine of pleasure as the chief good of life"?

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) Hedonist

• 'A person who believes in the doctrine of PLEASURE as the chief good' = Hedonist.
• Hedonism = philosophy that pleasure is the most important goal in life.
• Greek: 'hedone' = pleasure → hedonist = pleasure-seeker.
• A hedonist lives for maximizing pleasure and minimizing pain.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Hedonist = pleasure-seeker (hedone = pleasure in Greek)
• Related philosophical '-ist' words:
  - Hedonist = believes pleasure is supreme good
  - Ascetic = practices severe self-discipline, avoids pleasure
  - Stoic = endures pain/hardship without complaint, indifferent to pleasure
  - Idealist = believes in ideal principles over practical reality
  - Optimist = believes the best will happen
  - Pessimist = believes the worst will happen
  - Altruist = acts selflessly for others
  - Egoist = acts only for self-interest
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: One word substitutes for philosophical/character types — PSC regular.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. A hedonist spends money on luxuries and entertainment.
  2. The monk lived as an ascetic, the opposite of a hedonist.
  3. A stoic endures suffering without showing emotion.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) Ascetic
• Ascetic = avoids pleasure, practices austerity — OPPOSITE of hedonist.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Chief good' → philosophical word → Ascetic seems philosophical. But ascetic = avoids pleasure (opposite!).

(C) Stoic
• Stoic = endures difficulty without emotion — indifferent to both pleasure and pain.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Stoics also have a philosophy about pleasure — but stoic ≠ 'pleasure as chief good'.

(D) Idealist
• Idealist = believes in ideals/principles — unrelated to pleasure as chief good.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Doctrine/belief' → idealist feels like a believer in doctrines. But wrong doctrine!
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'HEDONI-ST = HEDONism = HONEY-IST — sweet pleasure seeker! HEDON = pleasure!'
  Hedonist loves pleasure like honey. Ascetic = opposite (austerity).
• Philosophical person types:
  Hedonist (pleasure) ↔ Ascetic (austerity) — learn as polar opposites!
  Stoic (indifferent to pain/pleasure) — middle path.
• PSC shortcut: 'pleasure as chief good' → HEDON-ist (hedone = pleasure). Root = answer.

questionText: Choose the correctly spelt word.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) Occurrence

• Correct spelling: O-C-C-U-R-R-E-N-C-E
• Key: double 'c' AND double 'r' — two sets of doubled consonants!
• 'Occur' → 'occurrence' — both 'c' and 'r' doubled.
• Rule: one syllable stressed before suffix → double final consonant (occur + rence = occurrence).
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: OCCUR + RENCE = OCCURRENCE (double c + double r)
  oc-CUR → oc-CUR-rence → add r (double the r before suffix)
• Doubling rule: When adding suffix to word ending in consonant:
  - If last syllable is stressed + ends in single consonant + single vowel before it → double the final consonant
  - occur (stress on 'cur') → occurred, occurring, occurrence (double r)
  - refer → referred, referring (double r)
  - begin → beginning (double n)
• Other tricky double-consonant spellings:
  - occurrence (double c, double r)
  - accommodate (double c, double m)
  - committee (double t)
  - necessary (one c, double s)
  - occasionally (double c, double s)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'Occurrence' — most tested difficult spelling in PSC.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. Flooding is a regular occurrence during monsoon.
  2. The strange occurrence puzzled the scientists.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(B) Occurence
• Single 'r' — 'occurence' missing the doubled 'r'. Should be 'occurrence' (double r).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Occur' has single r → students keep single r in 'occurence' — doubling rule ignored.

(C) Occurencee
• Single 'r' + extra 'e' at end — two errors. Neither correct.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Made-up distractor with obvious extra letter — eliminate first.

(D) Occurrance
• Double 'r' correct! But 'rance' instead of 'rence' — vowel wrong in suffix.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Gets the double 'r' right but misspells the suffix '-rence' as '-rance'.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'OCCURRENCE has TWO sets of doubles — double C and double R: OC-CUR-R-ENCE!'
  oCC-uRR-ence — two 'C's, two 'R's. Double double!
• Spelling breakdown: OC + CUR + RENCE — say it slowly: oc-cur-rence.
• PSC tip: 'Occurrence' — the only correctly spelled option will have both 'cc' AND 'rr'. Check both doubles!

questionText: Which word means "having a harmful effect"?

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) Pernicious

• 'Having a HARMFUL EFFECT' = pernicious.
• 'Pernicious' = causing serious harm, deadly harmful — extremely damaging.
• Latin: 'perniciosus' = destructive (per = through + nex = death/harm).
• 'Pernicious influence / pernicious habit / pernicious disease' — harmful in a serious way.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Pernicious = harmful, especially in a gradual/insidious way
• Pernicious vs similar words:
  - Pernicious = harmful effect (gradually destructive)
  - Perilous = dangerous, risky (immediate danger)
  - Precious = valuable, cherished (positive!)
  - Perceptive = having sharp understanding/insight (positive!)
• 'Pernicious' word family:
  pernicious (adj) → perniciously (adv) → perniciousness (n)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: One word substitutes for 'harmful' — pernicious vs perilous PSC-ൽ tested.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. Smoking has a pernicious effect on health.
  2. Social media can have a pernicious influence on young minds.
  3. The drug had pernicious side effects on the patient.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(B) Perilous
• 'Perilous' = dangerous, full of peril/risk — similar but not exact. 'Harmful effect' = pernicious (damage done). 'Dangerous' = perilous (risk of harm).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Pernicious and Perilous both start with 'Per-' and relate to harm — common confusion!

(C) Precious
• 'Precious' = valuable, dear — POSITIVE meaning, completely opposite to harmful.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Precious' starts with 'Pre-' and sounds like 'pernicious' — phonetic confusion.

(D) Perceptive
• 'Perceptive' = having keen insight/understanding — positive intellectual quality, not harmful.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: All four options start with 'Per-' or 'Pre-' — designed to confuse phonetically. Focus on meaning!
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'PERNICIOUS = PERNicious = PERNAMENT HARM — it harms over time, permanently!'
  Think: pernicious = per (through) + nicious (noxious/harmful) = harmful all the way through.
• Difference: Perilous = DANGER (you might get hurt). Pernicious = HARMFUL EFFECT (already hurting).
• PSC tip: All options start with 'Per-/Pre-' — ignore the sound, focus on meaning: 'harmful effect' = PERNICIOUS.

questionText:
Complete the sentence with the correct option.

If you had gone there, you ______

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) would have met

• 'If you had gone there' = Past Perfect clause — Third Conditional ആണ്.
• Third Conditional = past-ൽ happen ആകാതിരുന്ന ഒരു situation-ന്റെ imaginary result.
• 'You didn't go → so you didn't meet' — reality. Conditional = what WOULD have happened.
• 🔑 Formula: If + Past Perfect → would have + V3.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula — Three Conditionals:
  1st: If + Present Simple → will + V1 (possible future)
     'If it rains, I will stay home.'
  2nd: If + Past Simple → would + V1 (unlikely/imaginary present)
     'If I had money, I would buy it.'
  3rd: If + Past Perfect → would have + V3 (impossible past — didn't happen)
     'If you had gone, you would have met him.'
• Third Conditional clues: 'had gone / had studied / had tried' in if-clause.
• Result clause: would have + V3 (past participle).
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Third Conditional — PSC-ൽ most tested conditional type.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. If she had studied, she would have passed.
  2. If they had left early, they would have caught the train.
  3. If I had known, I would have helped.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) would meet
• 'Would meet' = Second Conditional result — but if-clause has Past Perfect ('had gone'), not Past Simple.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Would + V1' familiar pattern — students mix 2nd and 3rd conditional.

(C) will meet
• 'Will meet' = First Conditional result — present/future. If-clause Past Perfect-മായി match ആകില്ല.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Will' = future, meeting in future feel ആകും — but tense structure wrong.

(D) will have met
• Future Perfect form — conditionals-ൽ 'will have + V3' ഇങ്ങനെ use ചെയ്യില്ല.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Have met' part correct but 'will' wrong modal for 3rd conditional.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: '3rd Conditional = 3 PASTS — had gone / would have / met (V3)'
  If + HAD + V3 → WOULD + HAVE + V3
• Shortcut: See 'had + V3' in if-clause → answer is always 'would have + V3'.
• PSC pattern: Third conditional = regret about past. 'Had gone' in question = 'would have met' in answer. Guaranteed!

questionText: Pick out the correctly spelt word from the following.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) Receive

• Correct spelling: R-E-C-E-I-V-E
• Golden rule: 'I before E except after C'
  — after C → 'ei' (receive, deceive, ceiling)
  — elsewhere → 'ie' (believe, achieve, relief)
• 'Rec-EIVE' — after 'c', so E comes before I: E-I not I-E.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: 'I before E, except after C'
  - After C → EI: receive, deceive, ceiling, perceive, conceive
  - Elsewhere → IE: believe, achieve, relief, grief, piece, field
• Exceptions to remember (PSC trap words):
  - weird, seize, neither, leisure, foreign — 'ei' but not after 'c'
  - their, vein, reign — 'ei' for other reasons
• Etymology: Latin 'recipere' — re (back) + capere (to take)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: IE/EI spelling rule — PSC-ൽ repeatedly tested.
• Common '-eive' words (all after C → EI):
  receive, deceive, perceive, conceive, achieve (exception — no C!)
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. I received a letter from the PSC office.
  2. She achieved her goal through hard work.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(B) Recieve
• 'IE' after C — wrong! After C it must be 'EI'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Students apply 'I before E' blindly without the 'except after C' part.

(C) Reseive
• 'S' instead of 'C' — completely wrong spelling.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Phonetically, 'c' sounds like 's' in 'receive' — so some write 's'.

(D) Resieve
• Both 's' instead of 'c' AND wrong IE/EI order.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Double error — two mistakes combined to confuse.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'I before E except after C — reCEIve, deCEIve, CEIling!'
  After C → always EI. No exception for these words.
• PSC spelling family: receive / deceive / perceive / conceive — all -CEIVE (EI after C).
• Quick check: Is there a 'C' just before the IE/EI? YES → use EI. NO → use IE (believe, achieve).

questionText:
Fill in the blank using the appropriate form of the verb.

The government decided to abolish ______

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) rationing

• 'Decided to abolish ______' — 'abolish' ഒരു verb ആണ്, അതിന്റെ object വേണം.
• 'Abolish rationing' = ration system നിർത്തലാക്കുക.
• 'Rationing' (noun/gerund form) = ration ചെയ്യൽ — the system of rationing.
• 'Decided to abolish' + noun object = 'rationing' (the practice/system).
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: decide + to + V1 (infinitive) — 'decided TO abolish'
  Then 'abolish' takes an OBJECT (noun): abolish + what? = rationing (the system)
• Gerund as noun: '-ing' form used as noun:
  - 'Rationing' = the act/system of rationing
  - 'Swimming is good' (subject)
  - 'I enjoy swimming' (object)
  - 'The government abolished rationing' (object of verb)
• Verbs followed by infinitive (decide, want, plan, hope, refuse, agree):
  decide TO go, want TO eat, plan TO study
• Verbs followed by gerund (enjoy, avoid, finish, suggest, keep):
  enjoy swimming, avoid eating, finish working
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Infinitive vs Gerund after specific verbs — PSC-ൽ regular question.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The government decided to abolish rationing.
  2. She enjoys swimming every morning.
  3. He refused to accept the offer.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) ration
• 'Ration' = noun (a fixed portion) or verb. 'Abolish ration' grammatically possible but 'abolish rationing' (the system) more natural/correct.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Ration' is familiar word — students pick it without thinking about 'rationing' as a system.

(C) rations
• 'Rations' = plural portions of food. 'Abolish rations' = remove food portions, not the system.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Plural form feels more complete — but meaning slightly different.

(D) rationism
• Not a real English word — invented distractor.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: '-ism' suffix makes it sound like a valid concept word.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'DECIDE TO → then what? A THING (noun)!' Abolish WHAT? The SYSTEM = rationing.
• Key pattern: decide/plan/want/hope + to + V1 → then object can be a gerund-noun.
• PSC tip: When verb is followed by 'to + V1' and then another blank, think — is it a verb or a noun needed? Here 'abolish' needs a noun object = rationing.

questionText:
Select the appropriate question tag.

I am good, _______

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (C) aren't I?

• 'I am good' = POSITIVE statement, auxiliary = 'am', subject = 'I'.
• Positive → Negative tag needed.
• Special exception: 'I am' positive → tag is 'aren't I?' (NOT 'amn't I?' which doesn't exist in standard English).
• 'I am good, aren't I?' — ഇത് standard English-ലെ accepted form.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula — Special 'I am' tags:
  'I am' (positive) → 'aren't I?' ← EXCEPTION (amn't = not standard)
  'I am not' (negative) → 'am I?' ← regular positive tag
• Why 'aren't I?' not 'amn't I?':
  - 'Amn't' is not accepted in standard British/American English
  - 'Aren't I?' is the universally accepted exception
• General tag rule:
  Positive → negative tag | Negative → positive tag
  Same auxiliary in tag as in statement
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'I am' exception tag — PSC favourite because it's a known exception.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. I am your friend, aren't I?
  2. I am not wrong, am I?
  3. She is coming, isn't she?
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) am I?
• 'Am I?' = positive tag — used for NEGATIVE statements ('I am not..., am I?').
• Here statement is positive ('I am good') → negative tag needed.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Am' is in statement, so 'am I?' feels right — but polarity wrong.

(B) amn't I?
• 'Amn't' = not standard English. Grammatically invalid in formal usage.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Logically derived (am + n't = amn't) but not accepted usage — classic trap.

(D) isn't I?
• 'Isn't' = third person singular. 'I' never takes 'is/isn't'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Isn't it?' is common tag — students accidentally write 'isn't I?' forgetting subject-verb rule.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'I AM → AREN'T I? — AM becomes ARE for the tag!' Just remember this one exception.
• The two 'I am' tags — write them down once and memorize:
  I am → aren't I? | I am not → am I?
• PSC shortcut: See 'I am' (positive) in question → answer is 'aren't I?' — no thinking needed!

topic: Singular & Plural, Change of Gender, Collective Nouns
questionText: Choose the correct plural form of the word LOUSE.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) Lice

• 'Louse' = ഒരൊറ്റ പേൻ (a single parasitic insect).
• Plural = 'Lice' — irregular plural, '-ouse → -ice' pattern.
• Same pattern: Mouse → Mice, Louse → Lice.
• 'Louses' or 'lices' — both wrong. Only 'lice' is correct.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: -ouse → -ice (irregular plural)
  Mouse → Mice | Louse → Lice
• Irregular plural groups — PSC favourites:
  - -ouse → -ice: mouse/mice, louse/lice
  - -oot → -eet: foot/feet, tooth/teeth
  - -an → -en: man/men, woman/women
  - -ild → -ildren: child/children
  - -ox → -oxen: ox/oxen
  - Same form: sheep/sheep, deer/deer, fish/fish
  - Latin origin: cactus/cacti, syllabus/syllabi, radius/radii
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Irregular plurals — PSC-ൽ animal/insect plurals frequently tested.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The child had lice in her hair.
  2. Mice are found everywhere in old buildings.
  3. The oxen pulled the cart.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) Louses
• Regular '-s' added — wrong for irregular noun.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Regular plural rule applied to irregular noun — most common mistake.

(C) Lices
• 'Lice' is already plural — adding '-s' makes it double plural (wrong).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Students know 'lice' is related but add '-s' unnecessarily.

(D) Lousen
• Not a real English word — invented distractor.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: '-en' suffix exists (oxen, children) so 'lousen' sounds plausible — but wrong.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'MOUSE-MICE, LOUSE-LICE — they rhyme because they're the same family!'
  One mouse, many mice. One louse, many lice. Same pattern!
• Irregular plural pairs PSC loves: mouse/mice · louse/lice · foot/feet · tooth/teeth · child/children · ox/oxen
• PSC strategy: When you see irregular nouns — never add just '-s'. Think: does it follow a special pattern?

questionText: The antonym of 'mitigate' is ______

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) Aggravate

• 'Mitigate' = ദുരിതം/കഷ്ടം കുറയ്ക്കുക — to make less severe, reduce suffering.
• 'Aggravate' = കൂടുതൽ വഷളാക്കുക — to make worse, intensify.
• Exact opposites: mitigate (reduce) ↔ aggravate (increase/worsen).
• PSC context: 'Measures to mitigate flood damage' vs 'Actions that aggravate the situation'.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Mitigate = reduce/lessen ↔ Aggravate = worsen/intensify
• Word families:
  - Mitigate → Mitigation (n) → Mitigating (adj: mitigating circumstances)
  - Aggravate → Aggravation (n) → Aggravating (adj)
• Etymology:
  - Mitigate: Latin 'mitigare' = to soften (mitis = soft + agere = to make)
  - Aggravate: Latin 'aggravare' = to make heavier (ad + gravis = heavy)
• Synonym groups:
  - Mitigate = alleviate, lessen, reduce, relieve, ease, soften
  - Aggravate = worsen, intensify, exacerbate, inflame, compound
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Mitigate/Aggravate antonym pair — PSC-ൽ multiple times tested.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The medicine helped mitigate the pain.
  2. His rude reply only aggravated the situation.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(B) Alleviate
• 'Alleviate' = reduce/lessen — SYNONYM of mitigate, not antonym!
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Classic PSC trick — put a synonym as wrong option to confuse students who know mitigate = reduce.

(C) Stubborn
• 'Stubborn' = വാശിയുള്ള — completely unrelated to mitigate.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Random distractor for students who don't know mitigate's meaning.

(D) Lessen
• 'Lessen' = another synonym of mitigate — same meaning, not opposite.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Two synonyms (alleviate + lessen) placed as wrong options — tests if student knows antonym vs synonym.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'AGGRAVATE = GRAVE situation!' — aggravate makes things GRAVE (serious/worse).
  MITIGATE = MITI-gate = opens the gate for relief!
• PSC antonym trap alert: When you see synonyms of the word in options (alleviate, lessen) — eliminate them! Antonym = opposite.
• Word pair: Mitigate ↔ Aggravate — learn them as a pair, like optimism/pessimism.

questionText:
Use the correct preposition.

She is good ______ Mathematics.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തරം: (B) at

• 'Good at' = fixed preposition collocation — skill/subject-ൽ നല്ലത് ആകുക.
• 'She is good AT Mathematics' — ഇത് standard English expression.
• 'Good at' = ability/skill in a subject or activity.
• PSC context: 'He is good at English.' 'She is good at cooking.'
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: good AT (skill/subject) — always 'at', never 'in/on/for'
• Fixed preposition collocations — PSC must-know:
  - Good AT (skill): good at maths, good at cooking
  - Interested IN (topic): interested in science
  - Married TO (person): married to Raju
  - Afraid OF (thing): afraid of dogs
  - Responsible FOR (task): responsible for the project
  - Angry WITH (person): angry with him
  - Angry AT (situation): angry at the delay
  - Proud OF: proud of her achievement
  - Fond OF: fond of music
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Fixed preposition collocations — PSC-ൽ ഏറ്റവും frequent preposition question type.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. She is good at painting.
  2. He is not good at remembering names.
  3. Are you good at Mathematics?
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) on
• 'Good on' — not a standard English collocation.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'On' is common preposition, random choice.

(C) in
• 'Good in' — sometimes heard informally but NOT standard. 'Interested IN' is correct, not 'good in'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'In Mathematics' sounds natural (we study IN mathematics) — but 'good IN' wrong.

(D) for
• 'Good for' = beneficial for something ('Exercise is good for health') — different meaning entirely.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Good for' is a valid phrase but means 'beneficial', not 'skilled at'.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'AT = Aiming at a Target' — when you're GOOD AT something, you HIT the target!
• PSC preposition collocations cheat sheet:
  good AT · interested IN · married TO · afraid OF · responsible FOR · proud OF · fond OF
• Exam strategy: Memorize these 7 fixed pairs — they cover 80% of PSC preposition questions!

questionText:
Choose the correct article.

I saw _______ one-eyed man.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) a

• 'One-eyed man' — 'one' = 'w' sound (wun) — consonant SOUND ആണ്.
• Article rule: 'a' = before consonant SOUND, 'an' = before vowel SOUND.
• 'One' starts with 'w' sound (not 'o' sound) → 'a one-eyed man' ✅
• Similarly: 'a university' (yu- sound), 'a European' (yoo- sound) — vowel letter but consonant sound.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Article depends on SOUND, not SPELLING
  'a' = before consonant SOUND
  'an' = before vowel SOUND (a, e, i, o, u sounds)
• Tricky cases:
  - 'one' = /wʌn/ (w-sound) → a one-eyed man ✅
  - 'university' = /juːnɪ/ (y-sound) → a university ✅
  - 'hour' = /aʊər/ (vowel sound, h silent) → an hour ✅
  - 'honest' = /ɒnɪst/ (vowel sound, h silent) → an honest man ✅
  - 'European' = /jʊərə/ (y-sound) → a European ✅
  - 'MBA' = /em-biː-eɪ/ (em sound = vowel) → an MBA ✅
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: a/an with 'one', 'hour', 'honest', 'university' — PSC-ൽ classic questions.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. I saw a one-rupee coin.
  2. She waited for an hour.
  3. He is an honest officer.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) an
• 'An' = before vowel SOUND. 'One' = /wʌn/ consonant sound → 'an' wrong.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'One' starts with 'O' (vowel letter) — students see 'o' and write 'an' automatically.

(C) The
• 'The' = specific/already known. First mention of 'one-eyed man' — not specific.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: If the man was previously mentioned, 'the' would be right — but this is first mention.

(D) None of these
• An article IS needed here — 'I saw one-eyed man' without article is wrong.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'None of these' option confuses students who are unsure between a/an.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'ONE = WON (sounds like won) → starts with W sound → use A!'
  a one, a university, a European — all start with Y/W sounds despite vowel letters.
• PSC golden rule: LISTEN to the word, don't just READ it. Sound = king for articles!
• Tricky vowel-letter but consonant-sound words: one, university, European, uniform, unique → all take 'a'.

questionText: Past tense of shoot is _______

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) shot

• 'Shoot' = irregular verb — shoot → shot → shot.
• Past tense = 'shot' (not 'shooted' — that would be regular, but shoot is irregular).
• 'He shot the target.' — Simple Past of shoot.
• Same pattern as other irregular verbs: shoot/shot, sit/sat, hit/hit.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Shoot → Shot → Shot (V1 → V2 → V3 all different or V2=V3)
• Irregular verb pattern group — 'oo → o':
  - Shoot → Shot → Shot
  - Choose → Chose → Chosen
  - Lose → Lost → Lost
• Other important irregular verbs (PSC favourites):
  - Write → Wrote → Written
  - Ride → Rode → Ridden
  - Rise → Rose → Risen
  - Fall → Fell → Fallen
  - Fly → Flew → Flown
  - Blow → Blew → Blown
  - Draw → Drew → Drawn
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Irregular verb past tense — PSC-ൽ frequently asked.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The hunter shot the bird.
  2. She has shot three goals this season.
  3. He shot a film in Kerala.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) Shooted
• Regular '-ed' added to irregular verb — wrong.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Most verbs add '-ed' for past tense — students apply regular rule to irregular verb.

(C) Shooten
• Not a real English word — invented distractor.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: '-en' is a past participle suffix (taken, fallen) — 'shooten' sounds plausible but wrong.

(D) Shoten
• Also not a real word — another invented distractor.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Combines 'shot' + '-en' — looks like a past participle but not standard.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'SHOOT → SHOT — the 'oo' got SHOT and became 'o'!'
  Double-o (shoot) → single-o (shot) — bullet removed one 'o'!
• PSC must-know irregular verbs: shoot/shot · write/wrote · ride/rode · fly/flew · fall/fell · draw/drew
• Strategy: Never add '-ed' to these common irregular verbs — know them by heart. They come up in EVERY PSC paper.

questionText:
Choose the correct alternative.

Neither of us ______ coffee.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (C) likes

• 'Neither of us' = singular subject — 'neither' is always singular.
• Singular subject → singular verb → 'likes' (not 'like').
• 'Neither of us LIKES coffee' — both of us don't like, but grammatically singular.
• Rule: Neither/Either + of + plural noun → singular verb.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Neither/Either + of + noun → SINGULAR verb (always)
• Neither of us LIKES / Either of them IS / None of them HAS
• Compare:
  - Neither of us likes (singular — neither = not one of us)
  - Neither Raju nor Ravi likes (nearer subject rule — Ravi = singular → likes)
• 'Us/them/you' plural ആണ്, but 'neither of' makes it singular in meaning.
• ⭐ PSC ആවർത്തനം: 'Neither of us/them' SVA — PSC-ൽ regular question.
• Other always-singular subjects:
  - Each of the students HAS
  - Every one of them IS
  - None of them HAS (formal) / HAVE (informal — but PSC prefers HAS)
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. Neither of the boys has finished.
  2. Either of the answers is acceptable.
  3. Neither of us likes spicy food.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) like
• 'Like' = plural verb form — 'neither of us' singular ആണ്.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Us' is plural, so 'like' (plural verb) feel ആകും — but 'neither of' singular meaning ആണ്.

(B) liked
• Past tense — no past time reference in sentence. Present tense context ആണ്.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Coffee dislike = habitual fact → present tense. 'Liked' = past tense, wrong.

(D) liken
• 'Liken' = compare to something ('She likened it to a dream'). Completely different word.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Looks like a verb form of 'like' — but 'liken' = compare, not enjoy/prefer.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'NEITHER = NOT EITHER ONE = ONE = SINGULAR!' 
  Neither of us = not one of us → singular → likes.
• PSC pattern: 'Neither of ___' → always singular verb. No exception.
• Quick rule family: Neither of / Either of / Each of / Every one of / None of → all SINGULAR verb!

questionText:
Choose the correct preposition given in brackets.

He searched _________ the ring everywhere.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) for

• 'Search for' = fixed phrasal verb — എന്തെങ്കിലും തിരയുക.
• 'He searched FOR the ring everywhere' — ring-നെ തിരഞ്ഞു.
• 'Search for' = look for = try to find.
• PSC context: 'Police searched for the missing child.' — standard usage.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: search FOR (something) — always 'for', never 'of/to/in'
• Fixed verb + preposition collocations:
  - Search FOR = look for = hunt for (try to find)
  - Look FOR = search for
  - Wait FOR = expect
  - Ask FOR = request
  - Apply FOR = make application
  - Care FOR = look after
  - Hope FOR = wish for
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Verb + preposition fixed pairs — PSC-ൽ regular question.
• Compare search patterns:
  - Search FOR (something) = try to find
  - Search (a place) = examine a place (no preposition needed)
    'Police searched the house' vs 'Police searched for the thief'
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. She searched for her lost keys for an hour.
  2. He is searching for a new job.
  3. They searched everywhere for the missing file.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(B) of
• 'Search of' — not standard. 'In search of' is a phrase, but just 'search of' wrong.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'In search of' is a real expression — students drop 'in' and write 'search of'.

(C) to
• 'Search to' — no such collocation in standard English.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'To' is the most common preposition — random selection.

(D) in
• 'Search in' — wrong collocation. 'Searched in the bag' (location) possible informally but not 'search in the ring'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Everywhere' suggests location → students think 'in' fits.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'Search FOR = FOR-ever looking!' — you search FOR something that you haven't found yet.
• Collocations family: search FOR · look FOR · wait FOR · hope FOR · ask FOR — all take 'for'!
• PSC tip: Whenever 'search/look/wait/hope' appears in blank — answer is almost always 'for'.

questionText:
Choose the correct tense form of the verb given in brackets.

Our school team ___________ the cricket match last year.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (D) won

• 'Last year' = specific past time signal word → Simple Past tense.
• Simple Past = V2 (past form): win → won.
• 'Our school team WON the cricket match last year' — completed past action.
• 'Last year/last week/yesterday/ago' → always Simple Past.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: 'last year/week/month', 'yesterday', 'ago' → Simple Past (V2)
• Tense signal words:
  - Simple Past: yesterday, last week, last year, ago, in 2020, then, at that time
  - Present Perfect: just, already, yet, recently, ever, never, so far
  - Simple Present: always, usually, often, every day, generally
  - Future: tomorrow, next week, soon, in the future
• Win = irregular verb: win → won → won
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Signal word + correct tense — PSC-ൽ every paper-ൽ ഒരു tense question.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. She won the first prize last year.
  2. They won the championship three years ago.
  3. Our team won the match yesterday.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) had won
• Past Perfect — used for action before another past action. 'Last year' single past event ആണ്, so had won wrong.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Had won' sounds more formal/complete — students choose for 'better feel'.

(B) wins
• Simple Present — habitual action. 'Last year' past signal-മായി match ആകില്ല.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Team's general ability — 'our team wins' sounds natural in other context.

(C) win
• Base form — no auxiliary, grammatically wrong as main verb here.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Subject-verb: 'team win' (plural feel) — but team is singular collective noun anyway.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'LAST = PAST — last year, last week → always past tense!'
• Signal word strategy: Underline the time expression first:
  last year → past → won ✅
  just/already → present perfect → have won ✅
  tomorrow → future → will win ✅
• PSC shortcut: 'last year' = won, 'yesterday' = past tense verb — no thinking needed!

questionText: The children are playing, ___________?

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) aren't they?

• 'The children are playing' — positive statement, auxiliary = 'are', subject = 'children'.
• Positive → Negative tag needed.
• Auxiliary 'are' → negative 'aren't', subject 'children' → pronoun 'they'.
• 'The children are playing, AREN'T THEY?' ✅
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula:
  Positive + aren't/isn't/won't/can't...? (negative tag)
  Negative + are/is/will/can...? (positive tag)
• Subject → Pronoun conversion:
  - The children → they
  - The teacher → she/he
  - The book → it
  - Ram and Raju → they
• Auxiliary in statement = auxiliary in tag:
  - are playing → aren't (present continuous auxiliary = are)
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Present continuous question tags — PSC-ൽ regular.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. They are working hard, aren't they?
  2. She isn't coming, is she?
  3. The boys are playing, aren't they?
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) don't they?
• 'Don't' = Simple Present auxiliary. Statement uses 'are' (present continuous) — wrong auxiliary.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Children play' = Simple Present feel → 'don't they?' — but here it's 'are playing'.

(C) do they?
• Wrong auxiliary (do) + wrong polarity (positive instead of negative).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Double error — students who guess without checking get this wrong.

(D) weren't they?
• Past tense tag — statement is present continuous ('are playing'), not past.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'weren't' negative tag sounds right for 'children' but tense wrong.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'ARE → AREN'T, IS → ISN'T — just add N'T to flip!'
• Two-step check: ① Positive/Negative? ② Which auxiliary? → flip polarity, keep same auxiliary.
• 'Are playing' → 'aren't they?' — continuous tense tag uses 'are/is/am', not 'do/does'.

questionText:
Choose the correct passive form of the sentence given below:

People will support us.
optionA: They would be supported by the people.
optionB: We will be supported by the people.
optionC: They will be supported by the people.
optionD: He will be supported by the people.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) We will be supported by the people.

• Active: 'People will support US.' — 'us' = object.
• Passive: Object (us → We) becomes subject.
• 'We will be supported by the people.' — tense: will support → will be supported.
• 🔑 Formula: will + V1 (active) → will + be + V3 (passive).
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula:
  Active: Subject + will + V1 + Object
  Passive: Object → Subject + will + be + V3 + by + Agent
• Tense conversion:
  - will support → will be supported
  - will help → will be helped
  - will send → will be sent
• Object → Subject pronoun change:
  - us → We (object form → subject form)
  - him → He | her → She | them → They | me → I
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Future tense passive — PSC-ൽ active/passive conversion standard question.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. Active: They will invite us. → Passive: We will be invited by them.
  2. Active: She will complete the work. → Passive: The work will be completed by her.
  3. Active: People will elect the leader. → Passive: The leader will be elected by the people.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) They would be supported by the people.
• 'They' — wrong pronoun. Object was 'us' → passive subject = 'We', not 'They'.
• Also 'would' instead of 'will' — tense wrong.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'They' = people feel ആകും — but 'people' = agent (by the people), not subject.

(C) They will be supported by the people.
• Wrong pronoun — 'us' → 'We', not 'They'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'They' commonly used pronoun — students pick without checking object.

(D) He will be supported by the people.
• 'He' — singular pronoun for plural 'us'.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Random singular pronoun — clearly wrong but placed to fill options.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'Object becomes Subject — US becomes WE!' Object form → Subject form in passive.
  me→I, us→We, him→He, her→She, them→They
• Passive formula for future: will + V1 → will be + V3. 'Will support' → 'will be supported'.
• PSC check: ① Find object in active (us) ② Make it subject in passive (We) ③ Keep same tense (will be + V3).

questionText: If she had taken a car, she ___________ the station in time.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (A) would have reached

• 'If she had taken a car' = Past Perfect if-clause → Third Conditional.
• Third Conditional = impossible past situation — she DIDN'T take a car, so she DIDN'T reach in time.
• Result clause: would have + V3 (reached).
• 'Would have reached' — what WOULD have happened if she HAD taken a car.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula:
  3rd Conditional: If + had + V3 → would have + V3
  'If she HAD TAKEN → she WOULD HAVE REACHED'
• Third Conditional = regret/imaginary past:
  - Reality: She didn't take a car. She didn't reach in time.
  - Conditional: If she had taken... she would have reached...
• Signal: 'had + V3' in if-clause = always Third Conditional = always 'would have + V3' in result.
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Third Conditional — PSC-ൽ most tested conditional, appears almost every year.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. If he had studied, he would have passed.
  2. If they had left early, they would have arrived on time.
  3. If she had applied, she would have got the job.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(B) would reach
• 'Would reach' = Second Conditional result — but if-clause has Past Perfect ('had taken'), not Simple Past.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Would + V1' familiar pattern — students mix 2nd and 3rd conditional.

(C) had reached
• 'Had reached' = Past Perfect — result clause-ൽ Past Perfect ആകില്ല (if-clause-ൽ ആണ് Past Perfect).
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Mirrors the if-clause form — students copy 'had' pattern into result.

(D) will reach
• Future tense — Third Conditional result-ൽ 'will' ആകില്ല, 'would have' വേണം.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Reaching station = future event feel → 'will reach' logical seem ആകും.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'HAD in IF → WOULD HAVE in result — two HAVES make the 3rd conditional!'
  If + HAD taken → WOULD HAVE reached
• Pattern lock: See 'had + V3' in if-clause → pencil in 'would have + V3' for result. Always.
• PSC guaranteed: Third Conditional questions appear every year. Master this one pattern = free mark!

questionText: Choose the correct antonym of the word 'rigid'.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (B) Flexible

• 'Rigid' = കർക്കശമായ, വഴങ്ങാത്ത — unbending, stiff, not willing to change.
• 'Flexible' = വഴക്കമുള്ള, മാറ്റങ്ങൾ സ്വീകരിക്കുന്ന — able to bend, adaptable, opposite of rigid.
• PSC context: 'A rigid rule' vs 'A flexible policy' — exact opposite usage.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Rigid = stiff/unbending ↔ Flexible = bendable/adaptable
• Word families:
  - Rigid → Rigidity (n) → Rigidly (adv) → Rigidity
  - Flexible → Flexibility (n) → Flexibly (adv) → Inflexible (antonym)
• Etymology:
  - Rigid: Latin 'rigidus' = stiff, hard
  - Flexible: Latin 'flexibilis' = able to bend (flectere = to bend)
• Synonym groups:
  - Rigid = stiff, inflexible, strict, unbending, firm, unyielding
  - Flexible = adaptable, pliable, supple, elastic, accommodating
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: Rigid/Flexible antonym — PSC vocabulary regular.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. The new policy is more flexible than the old rigid rules.
  2. A rigid attitude prevents growth and learning.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) Gratify
• 'Gratify' = satisfy/please — unrelated to rigid/flexible.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Sounds positive (like something good), random distractor.

(C) Comfort
• 'Comfort' = ease/relief — not opposite of rigid.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Rigid = uncomfortable feel ആകും, so 'comfort' as opposite — wrong logic.

(D) Consent
• 'Consent' = agree/permission — unrelated to rigidity.
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Rigid person doesn't consent easily — students make this indirect connection.
explanation.tip:
💡 HOW COME Tip

• 🧠 Memory trick: 'RIGID = RIGID bar (stiff steel). FLEXIBLE = FLEX muscles (bend easily)!'
  Flex = bend → flexible = can bend = opposite of rigid.
• Word pair: Rigid ↔ Flexible | Stiff ↔ Supple | Inflexible ↔ Pliable — learn antonym pairs together.
• PSC strategy: 'Rigid' options will include unrelated words — eliminate by meaning. Only 'flexible' fits the stiff/unbending opposite.

questionText:
Choose the correct phrasal verb to complete the sentence.

My sister tries to ___________ some money every month.

explanation.correct:
✅ ശരിയുത്തരം: (C) set aside

• 'Set aside' = save/reserve money — ഭാവിക്കായി മാറ്റിവയ്ക്കുക.
• 'My sister tries to set aside some money every month' — monthly savings.
• 'Set aside' = reserve for a specific purpose (money, time, resources).
• PSC context: 'Set aside time for revision' / 'Set aside funds for emergencies'.
explanation.rule:
📌 വ്യാകരണ നിയമം

• 🔑 Quick Formula: Set aside = save/reserve (money/time) for later use
• All 'SET' phrasal verbs:
  - Set aside = save/reserve / ignore temporarily ('Set aside your differences')
  - Set off = start a journey ('They set off at dawn')
  - Set up = establish/start ('Set up a company')
  - Set in = begin (of weather/mood) ('Winter has set in')
  - Set on = attack ('The dog set on the stranger')
  - Set out = begin with purpose ('She set out to prove her point')
  - Set back = delay/hinder ('The rain set back our plans')
• ⭐ PSC ആവർത്തനം: 'Set' phrasal verbs — PSC-ൽ regular. Set off/up/aside most tested.
• ഉദാഹരണ sentences:
  1. She sets aside ₹2000 every month for emergencies.
  2. The committee set aside the old rules.
  3. They set aside time for daily revision.
explanation.wrong:
❌ തെറ്റായ ഉത്തരങ്ങൾ

(A) set on
• 'Set on' = attack or determined to do something ('He was set on winning').
• 🪤 PSC Trap: 'Set on saving' sounds like determined — but 'set aside' is the correct savings idiom.

(B) set off
• 'Set off' = begin a journey or trigger ('The alarm set off').
• 🪤 PSC Trap: Very common phrasal verb — students pick the most familiar one.

(D) set up
• 'Set up' = establish ('Set up a business'). Not used for saving money.
